question_text: The kharif cropping season in India is most accurately associated with which specific climatic period of the year?
explanation: The kharif season in India coincides with the south-west monsoon, beginning in June-July with the onset of the rains and ending with harvesting in September-October. Crops grown in this season such as rice, maize, cotton, jute and bajra are mostly heat-loving and require warm humid weather along with good rainfall.
subject: agriculture

options.text: Cool dry winter from October to March
South-west monsoon from June to October
Hot dry summer from March to June only
Post-monsoon period from October to December

elimination_hints.hint: Cool dry winter is the rabi season, not kharif
Hot dry summer is the zaid season
Post-monsoon overlaps with rabi sowing, not kharif

question_text: Wheat in India is grown principally in which specific cropping season of the agricultural calendar of the country?
explanation: Wheat is the most important rabi crop of India, sown in October-November after monsoon recedes and harvested in March-April. It requires a cool growing season with warm dry weather at maturity. Major wheat-growing states are Punjab, Haryana, Uttar Pradesh, Madhya Pradesh, Bihar and Rajasthan.
subject: agriculture

options.text: Kharif season during the monsoon months
Rabi season during the cool winter months
Zaid season during the dry summer months
Throughout the year as a perennial crop

elimination_hints.hint: Kharif season is for rice, maize, not wheat
Zaid is too hot for grain wheat
Wheat is annual, not perennial

question_text: Zaid season crops in India are grown during which specific climatic window between the major cropping seasons?
explanation: Zaid is a short cropping season between rabi and kharif, from March-April to June. It is essentially a hot, dry irrigated season for short-duration crops such as watermelon, muskmelon, cucumber, summer moong and fodder maize. Cultivation depends entirely on assured irrigation as natural rainfall is negligible during these months.
subject: agriculture

options.text: Between June and October during the monsoon period
Between October and March during winter months
Between March and June in hot dry summer months
Throughout the year in coastal areas of India

elimination_hints.hint: June to October is kharif, not zaid
October to March is rabi, not zaid
Zaid is a defined short summer season, not year-long

question_text: The Planning Commission of India had divided the country into how many major agro-climatic zones for planning purposes?
explanation: The Planning Commission of India divided the country into 15 agro-climatic zones based on rainfall, soil, topography and water resources, for the Seventh Five-Year Plan. The classification helps in resource-based agricultural planning and selection of suitable crops and cropping systems for each zone, accounting for India's vast climatic diversity.
subject: agriculture

options.text: 5 agro-climatic zones across the country
10 agro-climatic zones across the country
15 agro-climatic zones across the country
25 agro-climatic zones across the country

elimination_hints.hint: Five is too few for India's climatic diversity
Ten is below the official Planning Commission count
Twenty-five exceeds the Planning Commission classification

question_text: Cropping intensity for a given farm is most correctly defined by which specific quantitative formula in agronomy?
explanation: Cropping intensity is calculated as (Gross Cropped Area divided by Net Sown Area) multiplied by 100, expressed as a percentage. A value of 100 percent means each piece of land is cropped only once a year, while 200 percent means double cropping on the entire net sown area. Higher cropping intensity reflects more intensive land use through multiple cropping.
subject: agriculture

options.text: (Gross Cropped Area divided by Net Sown Area) multiplied by 100
(Net Sown Area divided by Gross Cropped Area) multiplied by 100
(Total Geographical Area divided by Net Sown Area) multiplied by 100
(Irrigated Area divided by Total Cropped Area) multiplied by 100

elimination_hints.hint: This inverted ratio gives a value below 100, not intensity
Geographical area is broader than cropped area
This is irrigation intensity, not cropping intensity

question_text: Multiple cropping in agronomy is most accurately defined by which specific cropping practice on the same piece of land?
explanation: Multiple cropping is the practice of growing two or more crops on the same piece of land within one calendar year, in sequence. The aim is intensification of land use to maximise productivity per unit area per unit time. Examples include rice-wheat sequence in northern India and rice-rice double cropping in the Cauvery delta of southern India.
subject: agriculture

options.text: Growing the same single crop year after year on one field
Leaving the field uncultivated for a complete year for rest
Growing two or more crops on the same land within one year
Growing different crops in completely separate plots in one year

elimination_hints.hint: Same crop year after year is monocropping, not multiple cropping
Leaving land uncultivated is fallow, not multiple cropping
Different crops in separate plots is not multiple cropping on same land

question_text: Intercropping in agronomy is best described as which specific spatial arrangement of two or more crops in a field?
explanation: Intercropping is the simultaneous growing of two or more crops in the same field in a definite row pattern, such as one row of maize alternating with two rows of soybean. The component crops differ in growth duration or canopy structure so that competition is minimised and resources like light, water and nutrients are used more efficiently than in sole cropping.
subject: agriculture

options.text: Sequential growing of two crops in succession on same land
Simultaneous growing of two or more crops in a definite row pattern
Growing of one crop in pure stand without any other crop
Random scattering of seed of two crops without rows

elimination_hints.hint: Sequential growing is sequence cropping, not intercropping
Pure stand is sole cropping, not intercropping
Random mixing is mixed cropping, not intercropping

question_text: Which of the following classifications correctly groups rice, wheat, maize, sorghum and barley together as one crop category?
explanation: Rice, wheat, maize, sorghum, barley, oats and rye are all cereals, members of the family Poaceae (Gramineae) that are grown primarily for their starchy edible grains. They are the principal source of food calories for humans worldwide. Pulses, oilseeds and fibre crops form separate functional categories of field crops.
subject: agriculture

options.text: Pulses (grain legumes) of family Fabaceae
Oilseeds rich in lipid storage in seed
Cereals belonging to family Poaceae (Gramineae)
Fibre crops grown mainly for textile use

elimination_hints.hint: Pulses include gram, pigeonpea, lentil, not rice or wheat
Oilseeds include groundnut, mustard, sunflower
Fibre crops are cotton, jute, mesta, not cereals

question_text: The rice-wheat cropping system is most extensively practised in which specific region of India by farmers?
explanation: The rice-wheat cropping system covers about 10 million hectares in the Indo-Gangetic Plains, including Punjab, Haryana, western Uttar Pradesh, Bihar and parts of West Bengal. Rice is grown in kharif and wheat in rabi on the same field, contributing the largest share of India's foodgrain production but also raising serious sustainability concerns about water and soil health.
subject: agriculture

options.text: Western Ghats and Konkan coastal region
Indo-Gangetic Plains of northern India
Deccan plateau of central southern India
North-eastern hill region of Assam and Meghalaya

elimination_hints.hint: Konkan region grows rice but not extensive wheat
Deccan grows jowar, cotton, and pulses primarily
North-east is jhum and rice-fish dominated, not rice-wheat

question_text: If a farm has Net Sown Area of 10 hectares and Gross Cropped Area of 18 hectares, the cropping intensity is correctly calculated as which value?
explanation: Cropping intensity equals (Gross Cropped Area divided by Net Sown Area) multiplied by 100. Substituting, (18 divided by 10) multiplied by 100 equals 180 percent. This means 80 percent of the net sown area was cropped twice and 20 percent only once during the year, indicating moderately intensive use of land.
subject: agriculture

options.text: 100 percent meaning single cropping on entire land
150 percent meaning fifty percent double cropping
180 percent meaning eighty percent double cropping
200 percent meaning complete double cropping of land

elimination_hints.hint: 100 percent would need GCA equal to NSA
150 percent would need GCA of 15 hectares
200 percent would need GCA of 20 hectares

question_text: Match the cropping seasons in Column A with the correct example crops grown in those seasons in India given in Column B.
explanation: Kharif season grows rice, maize, cotton and bajra; rabi season grows wheat, gram, mustard and barley; zaid season grows watermelon, muskmelon, cucumber and summer moong; and perennial crops include sugarcane and bananas grown for more than one year. Each season suits specific crops based on temperature and water needs.
subject: agriculture

elimination_hints.hint: Rice is not a perennial crop
Watermelon is not a kharif crop
Wheat is rabi, not kharif

question_text: Match the field crop classifications in Column A with their correct example crops listed in Column B for India.
explanation: Cereals include rice, wheat, maize and sorghum; pulses include chickpea, pigeonpea and lentil; oilseeds include groundnut, mustard and soybean; and fibre crops include cotton, jute and mesta. This functional classification helps in planning production targets and pricing policies under MSP.
subject: agriculture

elimination_hints.hint: Cotton is fibre crop, not cereal
Chickpea is pulse, not cereal
Groundnut is oilseed, not cereal

question_text: Match the cropping systems in Column A with their correct distinguishing features described in Column B for clarity.
explanation: Sole cropping is one crop in pure stand; intercropping is two crops grown together in a defined row pattern; mixed cropping is two crops sown together without distinct row pattern; and sequence cropping is one crop after another in succession on the same field within one year.
subject: agriculture

column_b.text: One crop after another in succession in same field
Two crops sown together without distinct row pattern
Two crops grown together in a defined row pattern
Single crop grown alone in pure stand

elimination_hints.hint: Sole cropping is not sequence cropping
Sole cropping is not mixed cropping
Sole cropping is not intercropping

question_text: Read the assertion and reason about kharif crops in India carefully and choose the most correct option from below.
explanation: Both statements are true and the reason correctly explains the assertion. Kharif crops such as rice, maize, cotton and jute are sown at the onset of the south-west monsoon in June-July because they require warm humid weather and high water availability for vegetative growth. The seasonal availability of monsoon rainfall in this period directly drives the kharif sowing calendar.
subject: agriculture
assertion: Kharif crops in India are sown with the onset of the south-west monsoon in June-July.
reason: Kharif crops require warm humid weather and high water availability for their vegetative growth phase.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: Water need is the very reason for monsoon-onset sowing
Kharif water need is well established
Monsoon-onset sowing of kharif is well established

question_text: Read the following assertion and reason about intercropping benefits carefully and choose the most correct option below.
explanation: Both statements are true and the reason correctly explains the assertion. Intercropping with a deep-rooted legume and a shallow-rooted cereal allows them to use water and nutrients from different soil depths and to use light through different canopy heights. This complementary use of resources gives a higher total yield from the field than from either crop grown alone.
subject: agriculture
assertion: Intercropping cereal with a deep-rooted legume often gives higher total yield than sole cropping.
reason: The two component crops use water, nutrients and light from different depths and canopy levels.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: Differential resource use is exactly why total yield rises
Differential rooting and canopy in intercrops is well established
Yield advantage of intercropping is well documented

question_text: Read the assertion and reason about cropping intensity in India carefully and choose the most appropriate option below.
explanation: The assertion is true but the reason is false. Cropping intensity in irrigated regions like Punjab is high because assured year-round irrigation supports two or even three crops per year on the same land. It is not because rainfall is uniformly distributed throughout the year — Punjab actually has a strongly seasonal rainfall pattern dominated by the south-west monsoon.
subject: agriculture
assertion: Cropping intensity in irrigated regions like Punjab is much higher than in rain-fed regions of India.
reason: Rainfall in irrigated regions like Punjab is uniformly distributed throughout the year supporting many crops.

options.text: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true but R is not the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: Punjab has highly seasonal rainfall, not uniform
The reason itself is climatically false
Punjab having higher cropping intensity is well documented

question_text: Arrange the following major operations of a typical kharif crop cycle in India in their correct chronological order from start.
explanation: A kharif crop cycle begins with land preparation by ploughing and harrowing in May-June. Sowing of seed is done at the onset of monsoon in June-July. Intercultural operations such as weeding, irrigation and plant protection follow during July-September. Finally the crop is harvested and threshed in September-October at maturity.
subject: agriculture

items.text: Land preparation by ploughing and harrowing in May-June
Sowing of seed at onset of monsoon in June-July
Intercultural operations and weeding in July-September

options.text: Q → R → S → P
P → Q → R → S
S → R → Q → P
R → P → Q → S

elimination_hints.hint: Harvesting cannot precede land preparation
Intercultural operations cannot precede sowing
Sowing cannot precede land preparation

question_text: Arrange the following months of major Indian cropping seasons in correct chronological order beginning with the start of kharif.
explanation: The Indian agricultural year follows kharif season starting in June-July with monsoon, then post-kharif harvesting in September-October, followed by rabi sowing in October-November and rabi harvesting in March-April. Zaid then occupies the short summer window from March-April to June, after which the cycle repeats.
subject: agriculture

items.text: Zaid season in March-April to June
Kharif harvest and rabi sowing in September-November

options.text: Q → R → S → P
P → S → R → Q
S → Q → R → P
R → P → Q → S

elimination_hints.hint: Zaid cannot start the kharif-rabi-zaid annual cycle
Rabi harvest in March cannot precede kharif sowing in June
Rabi sowing cannot precede kharif sowing

question_text: State whether the following statement about agro-climatic zones of India is TRUE or FALSE with proper justification given.
explanation: The statement is TRUE. The Planning Commission of India divided the country into 15 agro-climatic zones in 1989 for resource-based agricultural planning. The classification was based on rainfall, temperature, soil type, topography, water resources and existing cropping patterns, helping in zone-specific recommendations of crops, varieties and management practices for higher productivity.
subject: agriculture
statement: India has been divided by the Planning Commission into 15 agro-climatic zones for agricultural planning purposes.

options.text: TRUE — based on rainfall, soil, topography and water resources for zone-specific planning
FALSE — India has only 5 agro-climatic zones in total
FALSE — India has 50 agro-climatic zones in total
FALSE — India has not been classified into agro-climatic zones at all

elimination_hints.hint: Five is far below the official 15 zones
Fifty exceeds the Planning Commission count
India is officially classified into agro-climatic zones

question_text: Study the comparison table of kharif, rabi and zaid cropping seasons in India shown in the diagram and answer the question that follows.
explanation: The diagram shows that kharif crops are sown in June-July with the south-west monsoon and are mainly rain-fed, including rice, maize and cotton. Rabi crops are sown in October-November in the cool dry winter and are mainly irrigated, including wheat, gram and mustard. Zaid is the short hot summer season requiring full irrigation for crops like watermelon and summer moong.
correct_answer_text: Kharif crops are sown in June-July under the south-west monsoon and include rice, maize and cotton.
subject: agriculture

options.text: Kharif crops are sown in October-November during the cool winter only
Kharif crops are sown in June-July under the south-west monsoon and include rice, maize and cotton
Rabi crops are mainly rain-fed crops grown during the monsoon period in India
Zaid season needs no irrigation and is fully rain-fed across India

elimination_hints.hint: October-November sowing is rabi, not kharif
Rabi is mainly irrigated, not rain-fed monsoon
Zaid is fully irrigation-dependent in hot dry summer
